summary: This notebook provides an end-to-end inference pipeline for the Vesuvius Challenge Surface Detection task, demonstrating how to load 3D TIFF volumes, apply intensity normalization, and generate predictions using a pre-trained TransUNet model via the `medicai` library. It highlights practical techniques for handling large 3D medical images on limited GPU memory, including sliding window inference, 4x rotation test-time augmentation, and 3D morphological post-processing. Readers should learn how to efficiently deploy a segmentation model for submission while managing memory constraints and refining predictions through spatial post-processing.
competition_flow: Loads test TIFF volumes and metadata, normalizes intensities, runs sliding window inference with rotation TTA on a pre-trained TransUNet, applies 3D morphological post-processing, and packages the binary masks into a submission ZIP file.
data_reading: Reads test.csv for image IDs and paths.
Loads .tif files with tifffile.imread, converts to float32, and reshapes to (1, D, H, W, 1) for model input.
data_processing: Sliding window inference with 160³ ROI, 50% overlap, and Gaussian blending.
4x rotation TTA with inverse rotation alignment and averaging.
3D hysteresis thresholding, anisotropic closing, and small object removal.
ensembling: Averages model predictions across four 90-degree clockwise rotations, applying inverse rotations to align outputs before averaging.
insights: Sliding window inference with Gaussian blending is essential for processing large 3D volumes within limited GPU memory.
Rotation test-time augmentation effectively captures orientation-invariant features for 3D surface detection.
3D morphological post-processing, including hysteresis thresholding and anisotropic closing, significantly refines noisy segmentation masks for submission.
